source.title: The House of Wisdom
source.summary: In the 9th century, Baghdad had cheques, bills of exchange, international money transfers, and venture capital, all without interest.
source.author: Islamic Economics
source.body_text: In the 9th century, Baghdad had cheques, bills of exchange, international money transfers, and venture capital, all without interest. Explore how Caliph Harun al-Rashid built the world's first multicultural economic hub, how Abu Yusuf revolutionized tax policy, and how the hawala system moved money across continents on trust alone. Topics: Abbasid Baghdad, Harun al-Rashid, Abu Yusuf, Sakk & Suftaja, Hawala, Mudaraba Series: Historical Islamic Economics (Part I) • Episode 3 of 6

x.single_post: Baghdad in the 9th century had venture capital partnerships, international money transfers, and checks that moved wealth across continents. All without charging interest. https://islamiceconomics.github.io/podcast.html#ep-3
thread: Baghdad in the 9th century had venture capital partnerships, international money transfers, and checks that moved wealth across continents. All without charging interest.
The hawala system moved money on trust alone. A merchant in Baghdad could give gold to a money changer, and his business partner in Cairo could collect the equivalent without any physical transfer.
Abu Yusuf wrote the world's first treatise on public finance, advising Caliph Harun al-Rashid on tax policy that balanced state revenue with economic justice.
What we call modern finance was already flourishing in the Islamic world while Europe was still centuries away from banking. https://islamiceconomics.github.io/podcast.html#ep-3

linkedin.post: While Europe was still centuries away from banking, 9th century Baghdad had developed a sophisticated financial ecosystem that would make modern economists pause. The Abbasid capital under Caliph Harun al-Rashid featured cheques (sakk), bills of exchange (suftaja), international money transfers, and venture capital partnerships (mudaraba) - all operating without interest. The hawala system moved money across continents on trust alone. A merchant could deposit gold in Baghdad and have his partner collect the equivalent in Cairo without any physical transfer of funds. Abu Yusuf, the era's leading jurist, wrote the world's first systematic treatise on public finance, advising the Caliph on tax policies that balanced state revenue with economic justice. This wasn't primitive commerce - it was sophisticated finance guided by ethical principles. The Islamic world had created the infrastructure of global trade while maintaining its prohibition on exploitative interest. Listen to the full exploration of how Baghdad became history's first multicultural economic hub: https://islamiceconomics.github.io/podcast.html#ep-3
linkedin.comment_prompt: What parallels do you see between these ancient Islamic financial innovations and today's ethical banking movements?

The hawala system moved money on trust alone. A merchant in Baghdad could deposit gold and his partner in Cairo could collect the equivalent without any physical transfer. Abu Yusuf wrote the world's first treatise on public finance, advising on tax policies that balanced state revenue with justice. This wasn't primitive trade - it was ethical finance that connected continents. short_video.title: Baghdad Had Venture Capital in the 9th Century
short_video.hook: While Europe was in the Dark Ages, Baghdad had cheques and venture capital
short_video.voiceover: In the 9th century, while Europe was centuries away from banking, Baghdad had cheques, bills of exchange, and venture capital partnerships. All without charging interest. The hawala system moved money across continents on trust alone. A merchant could deposit gold in Baghdad and his partner could collect the equivalent in Cairo. Abu Yusuf wrote the world's first treatise on public finance, advising Caliph Harun al-Rashid. This was sophisticated finance guided by ethical principles, connecting the known world through trade.
